figures.caption: Fig. 1: Comparison of leading image fusion methods on TNO and RoadScene datasets. The results are evaluated based on average PSNR (dB), number of parameters (M) and complexity (GFLOPs). Note: GFLOPs values are shown on a log10 scale. The model size is depicted as the area of the ball.

Fig. 3: Detailed construction of the adaptive references in HLIF. Adaptive weights Zx and Zy are generated from source gradients through scaling, sigmoid mapping, and normalization. The generated weights are then used to construct the high-frequency gradient reference and the low-frequency luminance reference.
TABLE VII: Quantitative results (%) on the IVF for semantic segmentation task. The best and second-best values are marked in bold and underline, respectively.
Fig. 6: Visual comparison for “25026” in PET-MRI dataset and “3025” in SPECT-MRI dataset.
Fig. 5: Visual comparison for “soldier behind smoke 1” in TNO dataset and “FLIR 01415” in RoadScene dataset.
TABLE VI: Quantitative results on the MIF task. The best and second-best values are marked in bold and underline, respectively.

TABLE III: The details of baseline methods on MEF, IVF, and MIF tasks. “Para. (M)” represents the number of parameters in millions, “Time (ms)” denotes the average inference time. The best and secondbest values are marked in bold and underline, respectively.
TABLE I: The list of main notations used in this paper.
TABLE IV: Quantitative results on the MEF task. The best and second-best values are marked in bold and underline, respectively.
Fig. 4: Visual comparison for “Zentrum” in MEFB dataset and “SICE-Dataset Part1 12” in SICE dataset.
TABLE V: Quantitative results on the IVF task. The best and second-best values are marked in bold and underline, respectively.

Fig. 2: Workflow of CDNet. The Y-channel inputs are first expanded and concatenated as Z, then refined by iterative CDBlocks into the unified representation W . Each CDBlock is derived from the LISTA-style update of the combined sparse coding objective, where dD and dU follow the block-structured topology of D and DT to jointly update source-specific and common representations. The fused Y-channel is reconstructed from W with a residual connection from Z, and then guides chrominance fusion. The bottom inset shows the proposed HLIF loss, where adaptive weight generation produces Zx and Zy to construct weighted high- and low-frequency references for supervising ∇f and f through LHIF and LLIF , respectively.
